questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An X-ray beam is capable of being deflected
options: by both an electric field and a magnetic field
by neither an electric field nor a magnetic field
questions.solution:
X-rays are high-energy electromagnetic waves consisting of photons.

Since X-rays do not carry any electric charge, they do not experience any force in the presence of an electric field or a magnetic field.

Therefore, an X-ray beam is deflected by neither an electric field nor a magnetic field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a continuous X-ray photon emitted from a Coolidge tube. The energy of this photon is derived from
options: the kinetic energy of the free electrons in the target
the kinetic energy of the striking electron
an atomic transition within the target
the kinetic energy of the ions in the target
questions.solution:
Continuous X-rays are produced due to the deceleration of high-speed electrons when they strike the target material in a Coolidge tube.

The loss in the kinetic energy of these striking electrons is emitted as continuous X-ray photons, also known as Bremsstrahlung radiation.

Characteristic X-rays, on the other hand, are produced due to atomic transitions within the target atoms.

Thus, the energy of a continuous X-ray photon is derived from the kinetic energy of the striking electron.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a Coolidge tube, the energy of a characteristic X-ray photon is derived from
options: the kinetic energy of the free electrons in the target
the kinetic energy of the impinging electron
the kinetic energy of the ions in the target
an atomic transition within the target
questions.solution:
Characteristic X-rays are produced when an inner-shell electron of a target atom is knocked out by a high-energy incident electron, creating a vacancy in the inner shell.

An electron from a higher energy level of the target atom then transitions to fill this vacancy.

The energy of the emitted characteristic X-ray photon is exactly equal to the difference in energy between these two atomic levels.

Therefore, the energy of a characteristic X-ray photon is derived from an atomic transition within the target.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the potential difference applied to the tube is doubled and the separation between the filament and the target is also doubled, the cutoff wavelength
options: will become four times the original
questions.solution:
The cutoff wavelength of X-rays produced in an X-ray tube is given by the relation $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$.

The cutoff wavelength depends only on the accelerating potential difference $V$ and is independent of the separation between the filament and the target.

When the potential difference is doubled, $V' = 2V$.

The new cutoff wavelength becomes $\lambda'_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{e(2V)} = \dfrac{\lambda_{min}}{2}$.

Therefore, the cutoff wavelength will be halved.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the current in the circuit used to heat the filament is increased, the cutoff wavelength
questions.solution:
The cutoff wavelength (or minimum wavelength) of the continuous X-ray spectrum is given by:

$\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$

where $V$ is the accelerating potential difference between the cathode and the anode.

The current in the filament circuit only controls the temperature of the filament. An increase in filament current increases the number of electrons emitted per second due to thermionic emission, which increases the intensity of the X-rays produced.

However, it does not change the maximum kinetic energy of the electrons striking the target, which depends solely on the accelerating voltage $V$.

Therefore, the cutoff wavelength is independent of the filament current and will remain unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An X-ray beam emerging from an X-ray tube
options: contains all wavelengths shorter than a specific maximum wavelength
contains all wavelengths bounded between a minimum and a maximum wavelength
contains all wavelengths longer than a specific minimum wavelength
questions.solution:
The X-ray beam emerging from an X-ray tube consists of continuous X-rays (Bremsstrahlung) and characteristic X-rays.

The continuous X-ray spectrum has a sharp lower limit for the wavelength, known as the cut-off wavelength or minimum wavelength, given by $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$, where $V$ is the accelerating voltage across the tube.

There is no theoretical upper limit to the wavelength of the continuous X-rays produced, meaning the spectrum extends to longer wavelengths with decreasing intensity.

Therefore, the X-ray beam contains all wavelengths longer than a specific minimum wavelength.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Moseley's law describing characteristic X-rays is expressed as $\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - b)$. In this formula,
options: both $a$ and $b$ are dependent on the material
$b$ is independent of the material while $a$ depends on it
$a$ is independent of the material while $b$ depends on it
both $a$ and $b$ are independent of the material
questions.solution:
Moseley's law is given by $\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - b)$, where $\nu$ is the frequency of the characteristic X-ray and $Z$ is the atomic number of the target material.

The constants $a$ and $b$ depend only on the type of the spectral series (such as $K$-series, $L$-series, etc.) and the specific transition line within that series (like $K_{\alpha}$, $K_{\beta}$).

For example, for the $K_{\alpha}$ line, the screening constant is $b \approx 1$ and the proportionality constant is $a = \sqrt{\dfrac{3Rc}{4}}$. These values remain the same for all elements.

Thus, both $a$ and $b$ are independent of the target material.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The frequencies of $K_\alpha$ X-rays for various materials are measured. Which of the graphs in the figure could represent the relationship between the frequency $\nu$ and the atomic number $Z$?
questions.solution:
According to Moseley's law, the frequency $ u$ of the $K_\alpha$ X-ray is related to the atomic number $Z$ of the material by the equation:

$\sqrt{ u} = a(Z - b)$

where $a$ and $b$ are positive constants.

Squaring both sides, we get:

$ u = a^2(Z - b)^2$

This equation represents a parabola opening upwards with its vertex on the positive $Z$-axis at $Z = b$.

From the given figure, curve d is a parabola opening upwards, which matches this relationship. Curves a and c are concave downwards, and curve b is a straight line.

Thus, curve d represents the correct relationship.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Among the following wavelengths, one is absent while the rest are present in the X-rays emitted by a Coolidge tube. Identify the absent wavelength.
questions.solution:
In a Coolidge tube, the emitted X-rays have a continuous spectrum with a well-defined minimum wavelength, known as the cutoff wavelength $\lambda_{min}$, given by $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$, where $V$ is the accelerating voltage.

All wavelengths greater than or equal to $\lambda_{min}$ are present in the continuous X-ray spectrum, while wavelengths shorter than $\lambda_{min}$ are absent.

If only one of the given wavelengths is absent while the rest are present, the absent wavelength must be the shortest one among the given options. If a longer wavelength were absent, all wavelengths shorter than it would also have to be absent.

Among the given options of $25 \text{ pm}$, $50 \text{ pm}$, $75 \text{ pm}$, and $100 \text{ pm}$, the shortest wavelength is $25 \text{ pm}$.

Therefore, $25 \text{ pm}$ is the absent wavelength.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The provided figure illustrates the intensity–wavelength relations of X-rays emitted from two different Coolidge tubes. The solid curve corresponds to tube $A$, where the potential difference between the target and the filament is $V_A$ and the atomic number of the target material is $Z_A$. For the other tube, the respective quantities are $V_B$ and $Z_B$. Consequently,
questions.solution:
From the given graph, the cut-off wavelength (minimum wavelength) for tube A (solid curve) is smaller than that for tube B (dashed curve):

$\lambda_{min, A} < \lambda_{min, B}$

The cut-off wavelength is given by $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$. Since $\lambda_{min}$ is inversely proportional to the accelerating voltage $V$, we get:

$V_A > V_B$

Also, from the graph, the wavelength of the $K_\alpha$ peak for tube A is greater than that for tube B:

$\lambda_{K_\alpha, A} > \lambda_{K_\alpha, B}$

According to Moseley's law, the wavelength of characteristic X-rays is related to the atomic number $Z$ by $\dfrac{1}{\lambda} \propto (Z - b)^2$. A larger wavelength corresponds to a smaller atomic number. Thus:

$Z_A < Z_B$

Therefore, $V_A > V_B$ and $Z_A < Z_B$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Half ($50\%$) of the X-rays emitted from a Coolidge tube are able to pass through a $0.1 \text{ mm}$ thick aluminium foil. If the potential difference between the target and the filament is increased, the thickness of aluminium foil required to allow $50\%$ of the X-rays to pass through will be
questions.solution:
When the potential difference between the target and the filament is increased, the kinetic energy of the striking electrons increases.

This results in the emission of X-rays with higher maximum energy and shorter wavelength, as $E = eV = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{min}}$.

Higher energy X-rays have greater penetrating power, meaning their absorption coefficient $\mu$ decreases.

The intensity of X-rays after passing through a thickness $x$ is given by $I = I_{0} e^{-\mu x}$.

For $50\%$ transmission, $I = \dfrac{I_{0}}{2}$, which gives the half-value thickness $x = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$.

Since the absorption coefficient $\mu$ decreases with an increase in X-ray energy, the required thickness $x$ to allow $50\%$ of the X-rays to pass through will increase.

Therefore, the new thickness required will be $> 0.1 \text{ mm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Exactly $50\%$ of the X-rays emitted from a Coolidge tube are able to transmit through an aluminium foil of thickness $0.1 \text{ mm}$. If the potential difference between the target and the filament is raised, the fraction of the X-rays penetrating the identical foil will be
questions.solution:
The maximum energy of X-ray photons emitted from a Coolidge tube is given by $E_{\text{max}} = eV$, where $V$ is the potential difference between the target and the filament.

When the potential difference $V$ is increased, the energy of the emitted X-rays increases. These higher energy X-rays are known as hard X-rays and have a greater penetrating power.

The intensity of transmitted X-rays is given by $I = I_{0} e^{-\mu x}$, where $\mu$ is the absorption coefficient. As the energy of X-rays increases, the absorption coefficient $\mu$ of the material decreases.

Because $\mu$ decreases, the transmitted fraction $\dfrac{I}{I_{0}}$ increases.

Since initially $50\%$ of the X-rays were transmitted, the new fraction of X-rays penetrating the foil will be $> 50\%$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: X-rays from a Coolidge tube are incident on a thin aluminium foil, and the intensity of the transmitted X-rays is observed to be $I_0$. If the heating current is subsequently raised to increase the filament temperature, the new intensity of the X-rays transmitted by the foil will be
questions.solution:
Increasing the heating current increases the temperature of the filament in the Coolidge tube.

This leads to an increase in the rate of thermionic emission, causing more electrons to strike the target per second.

As a result, the number of X-ray photons produced per second increases, which means the intensity of the incident X-rays increases.

Since the accelerating voltage across the tube is unchanged, the maximum energy of the X-ray photons and the absorption coefficient of the aluminium foil remain constant.

The transmitted intensity is directly proportional to the incident intensity given by $I = I_{incident} e^{-\mu x}$.

Since the incident intensity increases while the fraction of transmission remains constant, the new transmitted intensity will be greater than $I_0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When visible light travels through a circular hole, it produces a diffraction disc of radius $0.1 \text{ mm}$ on a screen. If X-rays are transmitted through the identical set-up, the radius of the diffraction disc will be
questions.solution:
The radius of the central diffraction disc (Airy disc) produced by a circular aperture on a screen is given by:

$r = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda D}{d}$

where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of the light, $D$ is the distance from the hole to the screen, and $d$ is the diameter of the hole.

For a given experimental set-up, $D$ and $d$ are constant, which implies $r \propto \lambda$.

The wavelength of X-rays is much smaller than the wavelength of visible light ($\lambda_{\text{X-rays}} < \lambda_{\text{visible}}$).

Therefore, the radius of the diffraction disc produced by X-rays will be smaller than that produced by visible light.

$r_{\text{X-rays}} < 0.1 \text{ mm}$
